title: Prostate Segmentation Using Statistical Learning Models
content:
# Prostate Segmentation Through Simple Statistical Learning Models

Recently, as a part of my [Machine Learning in Medical Imaging](https://github.com/YipengHu/MPHY0041) course in UCL, I used principles of statistical learning to train a model tasked with automatically segmenting the prostate in T2-weighted magnetic resonance (MR) images.

The segmentation of biological regions of interest is crucial in medical imaging, as it can provide valuable insight on identifying anatomical and pathological structures. Automating this process through machine learning yields more consistent and efficient results, which enhance diagnostic precision and treatment planning.

This post delves into the approach I have followed to achieve binary segmentation of the prostate in MR images by leveraging supervised learning techniques, including preprocessing, feature extraction, model training, and evaluation.

![mri](segmentationmri.png)

## Background

The segmentation process involved classifying each pixel in an MR image as either a part of the prostate (foreground) or not (background).

### Dataset

The [PROMISE12](https://paperswithcode.com/dataset/promise12) dataset was used for this task. This is a dataset made available for the [MICCAI 12](https://promise12.grand-challenge.org/) prostate MR segmentation challenge

The dataset includes grayscale images with 128x128 resolution, and the corresponding ground truth is a binary mask of the same size.

The data, provided in the [PROMISE12](https://paperswithcode.com/dataset/promise12) archive, contained three sets of images: training, validation and test. Each set contained grayscale images, 128 x 128 pixels in size.

- The training set contained 30 MR images, paired with their respective ground truth masks.
- The validation and test sets each contained 10 MR images.

![promise12](promise12.png)

## Method

The first step involved extracting features out of each MR image. This is a common step in segmentation procedures, however it is especially crucial in medical images as they contain dense and complex structures which are not informative enough to interpret through raw pixel values. Extracting features like edges, textures, and frequencies can help classifiers learn better.

![image](mrifeatures.png)

The following filters were applied on the raw MR images:

| Filter                  | Process           | Purpose                                                                 |
| ----------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Scharr Filter           | Edge detection    | Highlighting boundaries of objects in  image, in this case the prostate |
| Difference of gaussians | Edge enhancement  | Highlights boundaries                                                   |
| Gabor filter            | Texture detection | Detects textural trends through varying frequencies and orientations    |
| Local binary pattern    | Texture detection | Captures local texture variations                                       |

```python
def preprocess_img(img, nchan=False):
    # Raw image and Scharr filter
    res = np.dstack((img, scharr(img)))

    # Add a series fo Gabor filters
    for frq in [0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8]:
        for j in range(4):
            a, b = gabor(img, frq, theta=np.pi / 4 * j)
            res = np.dstack((res, np.sqrt(a**2 + b**2)))

    # LBP
    radius = 3
    n_points = 8 * radius
    lbp = local_binary_pattern(img, n_points, radius, method="uniform")

    # Difference of gaussians
    dog = difference_of_gaussians(img, low_sigma=1, high_sigma=3)
    res = np.dstack((res, lbp, dog))
    return res
```

Instead of classifying each pixel independently, the method extracts small patches (local regions) around each pixel and trains a classifier on them. Each patch is a 21×21 region centered at a given pixel. This is done because a single pixel may not provide enough information, but a small neighborhood (patch) does. Through this process, the model learns contextual information around the prostate’s boundary.

```python
def create_training_set(train_path, n_train_patch, ps, chan, verbose=True, multi=True):

patch_dim = (ps, ps) # Dimension of square patch
patch_cnt = int((ps - 1) / 2) # Center of the patch
flat_dim = ps * ps * chan # Dimension of the "flattened" patch

Y = []
X = np.empty((0, flat_dim))

for s in np.arange(30):
    img_m, msk_x = rimg(train_path, s) # Binarize the mask
    msk = (msk_x > 0) * 1

    frames = preprocess_img(img_m) # Extract features
    frames = np.dstack((frames, msk)) # Add the labels as an additional channel

    patch = image.extract_patches_2d(frames, patch_dim, max_patches=n_train_patch) # Sample patches

    Y_tmp = patch[:, patch_cnt, patch_cnt, chan] == 1 # Each patch receives the label of the center
   # value in the "label" channel
    X_tmp = np.reshape(patch[:, :, :, 0:chan], (n_train_patch, flat_dim)) # Turn patches into a
   # matrix where each row corresponds to all the features of one patch

    Y.extend(Y_tmp) # Concatenate with Y and X
    X = np. concatenate((X, X_tmp), axis=0)
    gc.collect()
return (Y, X)
```

Next, the dataset is balanced. Medical images are often imbalanced, i.e. the region of interest is much smaller than the background. In this case, the prostate composes only around 20% of each image. This imbalance may lead to the model being biased towards the background class, hence the prostate pixels are oversampled to balance the dataset.

```python
def sub_sample(X, Y, sample_size=10000, ratio=None, verbose=True):

rtclass = np.sum(Y) / len(Y) # Compute class 1 frequency
if ratio is None:
    ratio = 1.0 / rtclass

weights = np.array([1] * len(Y))
weights[Y] = ratio
weights = weights / np.sum(weights)

idx = np.random.choice(X.shape[0], sample_size, replace=False, p=np.array(weights))
Xsub = X[idx, :]
Ysub = np.array(Y)[idx]

return Xsub, Ysub, idx
```

After the dataset was prepared, supervised learning models were trained and evaluated.

The first considered model was a [Support Vector Machine (SVM)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Support_vector_machine). SVM is a supervised learning algorithm that finds the optimal decision boundary (hyperplane) to separate classes in a high-dimensional space. The RBF kernel allows SVM to capture non-linear relationships by mapping data into a higher-dimensional space. A Support Vector Classifier (SVC) was considered for this application as it works well with high dimensional feature spaces.

The SVC was trained with a linear kernel, a polynomial kernel, and a radial basis function (RBF). While a linear kernel provides a simple decision boundary, polynomial and RBF kernels are better suited for capturing more complex relationships.

SVM regularization is controlled through the "C" values (hyperparameter tuning), where a lower C value indicates more generalization (high bias) and a higher C value indicates more fitting to training data (high variance).

```python
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

results = {"rbf": [], "poly": [], "linear": []}
for kernel in ["rbf", "poly", "linear"]:
    for C in [0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000]:
        svc = SVC(kernel=kernel, C=C, gamma="scale", degree=3, probability=True)
        svc.fit(Xsub, Ysub)
        y_proba = svc.predict_proba(Xsub)[:, 1]
        auc = roc_auc_score(Ysub, y_proba)
        results[kernel].append((C, auc))

    parameters = { # Parameters to be cross validated
        "kernel": ["rbf", "poly", "linear"],
        "C": [5, 7, 10],
        33
        "degree": [3],
    }

    svc = SVC(gamma="scale", probability=True)
    clf = GridSearchCV(svc, parameters, cv=5) # Cross validation
    clf.fit(Xsub, Ysub)

    : print(f"Best parameters found: {clf.best_params_}")
```

For evaluation, the following metrics were used:
- DICE: Measures overlap between predicted and ground truth masks
- ROC-AUC: Measures how well a model can distinguish between positive and negative classes

It was found that *SVC(kernel='rbf', C=7)* was the best model, which achieved ROC-AUC = 1.0 on training data. However, when this model was applied to validation images, this model scored DICE = 0.26 and ROC-AUC = 0.71, which indicates poor segmentation accuracy. This likely occured because SVMs struggle with large, high-dimensional data and do not generalize well in this case. As seen in the image below, the model predictions were generally very inaccurate.

![svm](svmpredictions.png)

The second considered model was a [Random Forest (RF) Classifier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Random_forest). RF is an ensemble learning method that builds multiple decision trees and combines their outputs to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting. It works well with high-dimensional, structured data and handles feature interactions effectively.

RF was considered as tree-based models handle high-dimensional feature spaces better than SVMs, and are more efficient on structured data (patch-based features).

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
rf_model = RandomForestClassifier()
rf_model.fit(Xsub, Ysub)
```

This method yielded marginally better results at DICE = 0.31 and ROC-AUC = 0.76, however it was significantly quicker to train. Hence, RF was used for the following segmentation operations.

As seen in the images below, although the RF classifier performs better than the SVC, the performance of the model is still insufficient.

![rf](rfpredictions.png)

To improve the performance of the RF model, hyperparameters were tuned for optimization. Varying sample sizes (500 to 5000 patches per image) and patch sizes (11, 17, 21, 27, 31 pixels) were explored. This was done since larger patches contain more context but may include background noise, and larger sample sizes improve generalization but increase training time.

![metrics](segmentationmetrics.png)

After training RF models with varying hyperparameters, it was found that a patch size of 31x31 pixels and a sample size of 5000 patches maximizes DICE, and a patch size of 27x27 pixels and a sample size of 2500 patches maximizes ROC-AUC. Since in the preliminary SVM and RF models the DICE score was considerably low, maximizing it through the patch and sample sizes was prioritized.

As seen in the images below, tuning the hyperparameters to a patch size of 31x31 pixels and a sample size of 5000 patches significantly improved the performance of the model:

![optimized](optimizedpredictions.png)

A final step to further improve the performance of the model is image post-processing. Morphological operations can be done through the [scikit-image](https://scikit-image.org/) library to eliminate small patches of incorrect labels and generalize the prediction into the area of the largest prediction patch.

The applied morphology strategy is as follows:

- **[Erosion](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.morphology.html#skimage.morphology.erosion):** This ensures smaller clumps and outliers are eliminated and only the large clump near the prostate is left. This yields a much smaller mask compared to the original and thus the next steps are focused on using the current mask as an “origin” and expanding upon the mask
around this area.

- **[Convex hull image](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.morphology.html#skimage.morphology.convex_hull_image):** This confines and merges all remaining points within a polygon. This fills in any gaps created within the region of interest due to erosion and acts as a basis for further widening the mask.

- **[Dilation](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.morphology.html#skimage.morphology.dilation):** This increases the size of the mask to compensate for the missing sections due to erosion.

Further methods were experimented with (such as only considering the largest clump of pixels and different [skimage.morphology](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.morphology.html) methods) however the greatest success was found with this method.

As seen in the images below, post-processing significantly improved segmentation quality:

![morphology](morphology.png)

## Evaluation

The final model was applied to the test data to evaluate the performance of the model.

![test](testdata.png)

The test dataset yields noticably higher accuracy compared to the validation dataset. Hence, it could be suggested that the model under ideal conditions is not overfit. However, as a result of postprocessing, the results are very rounded which decreases accuracy.

This results in high variance and low bias, making it relatively accurate for interpreting a large test dataset. However, for a clinical application an average DICE score of 0.76 could be too low and may suggest too much variance in the model.

As a suggestion for further work, the preprocessing methods could be improved. No cross validation was done while choosing the model for the segmentation model (due to computational constraints), and it could yield better results before morphology is applied. Additionally, a more accurate and robust model would rely less on postprocessing.

## Reflection and Future Work

This project was a defining experience during my time at UCL, and I was fortunate to recieve a 91% mark for it. I have always been drawn to the intersection of engineering and computational methods, and this work reinforced that passion. Applying statistical principles to build machine learning models capable of segmenting anatomical structures in MR images was both challenging and rewarding. It was a hands-on opportunity to bridge theory with application, which deepened my appreciation for data-driven approaches in biomedical engineering.

I am currently working on another project as part of this module, which utilizes semi-supervised deep learning to classify features in pelvic MRI. The opportunity to explore deep learning in a low-label setting is incredibly exciting, and I’m eager to see how it compares to the more traditional methods used in this project.
